speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is Boris Chartan. The interview is taking place in Plainview, New York, United States of America. I'm interviewing him. My name is Anne Bernard. And the interview is taking place in English. What happened when you went back to the office?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me, I watch you, he says, quite often at that window. What is it that fascinates you? I says, you know, Mister, I'm going to be very truthful with you. I keep thinking, will I ever be able to afford it, to buy anything in this type of a store? And he smiled and he says, of course you will. I said, but I can't fathom this thing, making $12 a week. And probably by then already I was maybe making $18 or $15, you know. He smiled. And he says-- ah, he says, you'll do all right. He says, have faith. You came through a lot. And probably about two weeks later, he called me in and he asked me to go out and do the selling. I was a little taken back. And he gave me addresses and they give you a case to carry and to go up. And I started going up on 1400 Broadway, 575 Seventh Avenue, 1380 Broadway, 1375 Broadway. These were mainly the house, knocking on doors, and you know, very little of the language. And it wasn't easy. And until they take some samples from you, you didn't get an order and I felt bad, because I know he wasn't-- I wasn't bringing in enough business. And he says, don't worry about it. You just keep going. And so one day I was in the cafeteria on 37th Street and Broadway. I think it was Garfield's. And salesmen used to come in there. And there was-- there was always sort of couple survivors already, some were maybe in the dress salesmen. And they all had a conversation that the buyer at George Hess and Company is a Polish fella. The name was [? Stalleen. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I concocts in my mind that I'm going to go up there. But you couldn't go up the front door. Salesmen were never permitted to go up in the front elevators in these buildings in those days. I went up through the service elevator, knocked on the-- rang the bell. And he says, who's there? And I answered him in Polish. So he was probably like taken and he wanted to see who this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: He opened the door. And I came him. I gave him my sob story. He listened. And I tell him that I went through the war, and the opportunity if he can give me to look at my line at least and see if he can buy from Streamline. He immediately proceeded to call in the pattern maker, a nice little old lady that came in. She looked at the line. He explained who I am, why I'm here. And they gave me-- they ordered some samples, which is just to try out. I ran back excited and told my boss. I says-- not the immediate boss, Mr. [? Ali ?] you didn't see. Fella by the name of Johnny Meltzer, which was a very fine man too, guided me. And I said to Johnny, this is it. And Johnny was very excited and he says, everybody, drop everything. Give Boris the samples. Let him go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back and brought them there, and that was my first sale. After that plugging around New York, he had asked me would I go out of town. And I says, where? He said, you go to Boston two days and you go to Philadelphia two days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was afraid to say no, and I said yes. But then when I went home, I was really frightened. I'd never been to-- outside of New York anyplace. So my first trip Monday morning was to go to Philadelphia. I got up at-- very early morning, went down to Penn Station. Anyhow, they also told me that they made reservations for me at the Benjamin Franklin Hotel for Monday night. So I got into Philadelphia, got off the train. Never forget the Philadelphia station. And I had to take a train to go to-- I don't-- it wasn't Jack Squire. It was [? Leglan ?] Apparel. That was the name of the company, [? Leglan ?] Apparel in Philadelphia. And got on this train. Got off there, saw this factory. Walked in there and a young lady opened the door and I went in. I told her who I am. She saw my English was bad. She sort of asked me a few questions. And I said to her, you know, in your hands you control my future. If I can do well, maybe I can make something out of myself. She listened. And I came back from Philadelphia with a very, very big order. And came back on Tuesday night. Wednesday morning I went into the place, brought my orders in. Thursday and Friday I went to Boston. And in Boston, I went into the coathouse Goodstein Brothers. They were making coats in Boston. And that's how I started selling. The only next thing that I recall is again I was living very, very frugally on my travels. And the controller of our place was Mr. [? Arliss ?] and Mrs. [? Cohen, ?] a very fine lady, always her hair very neat, combed. Again, I get called in on a Friday and somebody says Mrs. [? Cohen ?] wants to see you. I figured, oh, getting my last paycheck, and you know, you're parting friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she says, sit down. I sat down. She says to me, tell me, when you travel, do you eat anything? And I says, yes, [? Mrs. Cohen. ?] She says, you satisfied? I says, absolutely, no problem. The hotel was paid by them. And she says, but how can you spend so little money? She says, you know-- they had a very hot-shot salesman, a nice guy. She says, when Herman walks from here to Seventh Avenue it's more than what you spend for two days. Well, that's Herman, I says, and that's me.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, no. She says, you gotta do a little better. I know you don't eat the right meals when you travel, because you cannot buy it for the-- just what you put in. I thanked her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay at Streamline?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed at Streamline 'til about 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was talking to Mr. [? Ardler. ?] And it was a-- even though with all the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excitement of being a salesman and being in the garment industry, I wasn't happy. So I had gone in and spoken to him. And he said to me, you-- you should start looking towards getting into a business where you'll be your own boss, he says. From me watching you, that's the best advice I can give. He said, we're not firing you. We're not telling you to leave here. But you would probably eventually be much happier if you can find a business where you'll be the boss of it, he says.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided to-- my family, the Chartan Family, some were on the building making his business, window cleaning. I went to a cousin of mine by the name of Harry Chartan. And I said to him, I'd like to get into that business. My dad was working as a window cleaner at that time. So I-- he made arrangements and I took off that summer. I'll never forget it. One summer I didn't work. And I just took a six weeks vacation doing nothing, just going to Brighton Beach every day, getting a nice tan. [LAUGHS] Had the best tan of my life, probably.

speaker: interviewee
text: And went into my own business. Took my dad out of where he was working and moved onto Mineola, where we bought a business, a maintenance business, and developed it all these years, stayed with it 'til 1982 from about 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the money to buy the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was borrowed, strictly all borrowed. Paid It back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife going to a dance at [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Diplomat Hotel. And what had happened that night, we used-- we used to live on the East and I had a lot of friends, Americans, European. And Saturday night was a big night. Everybody went to a dance some place. An some of my friends decided to go someplace to Jersey, which I didn't. I walked into the Diplomat Hotel with some of my friends. I had met her. We probably danced once. We said hello to each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And said to-- I don't know why and she never [INAUDIBLE] up to me. I says, Walter's going to take you home tonight. She says, what? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, my friend Walter. She went home with Walter. I had her telephone number. And then I called her. Not that she-- just some or other. They hung around longer that night. And I thought, well, if Walter feels that ways, it's OK. He's a very good, dear friend of mine. And she went home with Walter. I called her probably during the week for a date and we started dating.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Got married in 1951, which is 45 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three, three sons. And now I have two sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have four grandchildren, two from my older son, Steven, which is no longer here, and two from my middle son, Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do now, Boris?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the commissioner of the Department of General Services for Nassau County in my job. And my volunteerism I've spent almost [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 30-some-odd years besides working and making a living, I felt that as a survivor I should be involved in a lot of Jewish causes. I'm a conservative Jew. I was [INAUDIBLE] president of my temple. I was the president of the temple, the Plainview Jewish Center. I moved on to become the president of the metropolitan region of United Synagogue for Conservative Judaism, which covers the entire metropolitan region, which is Queens, Nassau, Suffolk, Westchester, Orange, and Rockland county. It's 120 and some-odd congregations. I finished my two years there and I went on to become a national vice president for Conservative Judaism. I've been involved with the Israel bond-- bonds. I'm on a cabinet here on Long Island, Israel bonds. I had a yearning and a very strong feeling as a survivor [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the Holocaust has to be taught in some form and fashion. While I was here in Plainview, I established a beautiful memorial that was built. And that was great, but I felt memorials is not the answer to teaching the Holocaust. It's just a figure. It means nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So about a number of years I started talking up about it and I'm also involved in the political-- in the Republican Party in Nassau for a span of-- I don't know if it's to boast about it or not, but [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I met a lot of good friends in the Republican Party through the years, a lot of nice people. Well, we think that politics are dirty, but there are also a lot of nice clean people and you make good friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was able to convince then the county executive, Frank Purcell, that there's a need for an educational center in Nassau. He bought it We established a commission. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some or other, it didn't go fast enough. But then Purcell went out and Tom Gulotta became the county executive. And I went to him and I asked him that this is part of what has been and we'd like to continue. Would he-- would he be in favor for it. He said, absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: He revitalized this commission. And I did not want it to be a religious type of learning institution that-- so I immediately-- he asked me, what would you see? I says, I think it should take the bishop, McGann, and Rabbi Fenster, appoint them as the commission chairman. And then we'll put on Jews and non-Jews alike on a commission. And we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the commission made a study [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that there's need for educational center. And we started looking for a place. We thought that we were going to build a facility in Eisenhower Park, but that became impossible because it's parkland and you can't take away a parkland without going through a process of four assembly votes and this whole thing. And that, I knew we had no more time left. Survivors were getting older.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at one point, the facility in Wildwood became available to Pratt Estate, which is an-- it's a passive park, called. It's on 204 acres of land and there's a 37-room mansion. The county has offered that mansion to us. We had established a nonprofit organization, which is the Holocaust Memorial and Educational Center of Nassau County, so the citizens are not burdened with the-- with the tax money to be spent on it. That's strictly from private donations.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was elected president. I've been president. I'm still president. And a lot of good people, and throughout Nassau and Suffolk that support us. It's a-- it's a marvelous institution. We're geared just to bring school children and we have survivors, educators to talk to them. We their films. We have a library now with over 5,000 books. We only have one paid professional right now. The rest are all volunteers. We have a librarian that's got a PhD in library-- a librarian. She works for the Federation, Jewish Federation of New York. She just retired. She came to work for us free. I have a survivor that is the educational director, and many other people and people that you'll meet here later that are not survivors that I've met through-- what I say-- we were both saying Kaddish at the same time. And we was meeting every morning in temple. She had lost her mother. I at that time-- I think at time I was saying Kaddish for my mother-in-law. My-- my mother-in-law didn't have any sons and I figured that the right thing would be for me to say Kaddish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we started to become friendly, you know, every morning and talking and tell them about the Holocaust and Center. And they came. They saw. And they chose to be involved with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the-- her husband Neil is the treasurer. And she's-- we call her the executive administrator, which we don't pay here. She's got a big title, but she's doing all the work on a daily basis. And what is so nice to see is that they have two sons. The entire family became involved with the Center. And that's ongoing. And this is what I felt as a survivor, just like the Spielberg foundation I realize is doing a great job, videotaping it and everything they're involved with. We too are trying to bring the center strictly to be educational. This year we had our second seminar how to teach the Holocaust. From all the public-- 56 school districts in Nassau, we were able to bring in 53 school districts. We brought in professionals to tell these teachers how to teach the Holocaust. So-- but that is an ongoing thing. I hope that it will be going on for a long time. The community is responding. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Good people come forward. They fund it. They see that we have the wherewithal to do this. Of course, we could do much more. I'm not going to make a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pitch here for Steven Spielberg. If he ever watches the thing, all I'd like for him is also to come and see what our center looks like, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to tell the story today of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided because the first [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] probably 30 years, survivors could not talk about it. We were just probably night dreaming. The dreams are horrible. And we just kept it all bottled up in ourselves. But telling the story, I feel that perhaps [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we've done some videos ourselves. We share with the children the content of something. And I watch their eyes and how they're glued to it. But they can really feel they touch a survivor. They get a real person that-- that's telling a story, rather than just book writings.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that if this film too will be shown someplace, whether it's kids are brought in from a school or what, that that's going to be very important, not only to remember what has happened to us six million, million and a half children lost, but to be on guard that things like this will not repeat themselves. Will it help? It's hard to tell. I don't know. But that's why I thought that maybe my story also should be told that way. Is there anything else that you'd like to add to what you've already said?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say that having a understanding wife for 45 years gives me the opportunity to go out and be able to tell this story. And she gives me all the time that I need to do my volunteer, like it's very understanding on her part, and I'm grateful for that. [CRYING] After all the suffering that she went through, losing her son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is my grandparents, Perl and Israel Chartan. My grandfather probably had the biggest influence on me, Israel Chartan. They both died before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Podkamien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This would be in the year 1933. This picture is of my mom and dad, David and Golda Chartan. This picture was taken in this house, or perhaps it could be their apartment. And that was taken in the year 1975 or '76. This picture was taken in 1935 in Dubno. From left is my mother's sister Sara, then her other sister Sasha. And in the back it's me. And on the right, it's Sara's husband, Uncle Morris. We're in a kayak on a river, [INAUDIBLE]. That is my mother's sister Sara, taken in Dubno. And the year would be 1936. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She was pregnant at the time, had her first church, the only child they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Got killed with her and her husband in Dubno. These are two friend-- my friend and his mother from Podkamien. They both survived. She's passed on since. He lives in California. His name is [? Zosio. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Dubno. And it would be in 1945. This is a picture of Russian soldiers and some volunteers that volunteered to go into the Russian army. This is a nephew, the-- next to the girl, the second one from the left, of my aunt Sara's sister-in-law. His name was Jacob Gaia. And that was taken right in Dubno of a group of these Russian soldiers. This is a picture of my friends in Fohrenwald, all survivors, taken in the DP camp. And that would be in 1944-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] no. '45, 1945. And I am there the second from the right, with the bushy hair, holding my arm around the last fella. This is a picture taken of Fohrenwald of a group. It probably had to be like the armor parade. And these were Zionists, the Betar. And probably most of these fellas wound up in Israel. This picture I took in Bremerhaven when we were boarding the marine in July of 1946 to go towards America. It's all refugees boarding that boat. This is a picture of my Pidyon ha-Ben of my first grandson, taken at the Plainview Jewish Center here. We had the Pidyon ha-Ben here in Plainview. That's me, my dad, my little grandson there, my son Steven, and his wife Amy. And that was taken 15 years ago, which would be in 1980-- 1980.
